Topics/Ault-Tallman-Branch-Summary.title: Ault Tallman Branch Summary
Topics/Ault-Tallman-Branch-Summary.content:
Ault and Tallman Branch Summary
This branch is a strong visitor path because it has a clear household story: Ohio Ault records, Iowa Tallman farming households, and later Soldiers’ Home and cemetery evidence. It also connects to the landing-page Tallman monument image.
Branch Diagram

graph TD
  FA["Frederick Ault<br/>1795-c.1857"]
  EPA["Elizabeth Plomey Ault<br/>1841-1926"]
  MMT["Miller Mathias Tallman<br/>1841-1921"]
  B1745["Benjamin Tallman<br/>1745-1820"]
  JT["John Tallman<br/>c.1788-c.1855"]
  EH["Elizabeth Harrison<br/>wife of John Tallman"]
  NH["Nathaniel Harrison<br/>d. 1807"]
  MW["Mary Woodley<br/>b. 1764"]
  WT1720["William Tallman<br/>1720-1791"]
  AL["Ann Lincoln<br/>1725-c.1812"]
  BT["Benjamin B Tallman<br/>1811-1881"]
  RM["Romancy Miller<br/>1819-1911"]
  WG["Willis G. Tallman<br/>William Gilmore?<br/>b. ~1874"]
  LT["Lenore Hetty Tallman<br/>Elvin Lenora?<br/>1879-1953"]
  UBT["Uriah Blake Thorpe<br/>1878-1959"]

  FA --> EPA
  EPA --- MMT
  MMT --> WG
  MMT --> LT
  EPA --> WG
  EPA --> LT
  LT --- UBT
  B1745 --> JT
  NH --- MW
  NH --> EH
  MW --> EH
  EH --- JT
  WT1720 --- AL
  WT1720 --> B1745
  AL --> B1745
  JT --> BT
  BT --- RM
  BT --> MMT
  RM --> MMT

The solid family group around Elizabeth Plomey Ault and Miller Mathias Tallman is documented in census summaries. The Benjamin/Romancy-to-Miller Mathias link is now supported by the Miller genealogy page image and the Thorpe pedigree timeline, but still needs primary-record confirmation.
Start With These People

Elizabeth Plomey Ault - Ohio-to-Iowa life progression from 1850 through 1920.
Miller Mathias Tallman - Elizabeth’s husband, Iowa farmer/teamster and later Soldiers’ Home resident.
Frederick Ault - Ohio Ault household head and father of Elizabeth Plomey Ault.
Benjamin B Tallman - Iowa Tallman farming patriarch with 1850-1880 household coverage.
Romancy Miller - Tallman matriarch tracked across six decades of household records.
Benjamin Tallman 1745-1820 - Older Tallman ancestor linked by burial, pedigree, and pioneer-book evidence.
William Tallman and Ann Lincoln Tallman - Older Tallman generation identified in the Durfee genealogy as Benjamin’s parents.
John Tallman and Elizabeth Harrison - Older Ohio Tallman couple now tied to Harrison/Woodley compiled-book evidence from Rockingham County, Virginia.
A Western College history names a Benjamin Tallman as farm agent and executive-committee member, but the page does not identify which Benjamin Tallman this was.
Samuel Tallman and Sarah Wells Tallman - Fairfield County pioneer context for the Tallman settlement cluster.

What We Know

Elizabeth Plomey Ault appears as a child and young woman in Ohio Ault households, then as Elizabeth Tallman in Iowa census records.
Miller Mathias Tallman and Elizabeth are documented in 1880, 1900, 1910, and 1920 census summaries, including Iowa Soldiers’ Home records.
Benjamin B Tallman and Romancy Miller are documented across Iowa household records from 1850 through 1880, with Romancy continuing into later widowhood records.
The Miller genealogy page image identifies Miller Mathias Tallman as a child of Romancy Miller and Benjamin Tallman, reducing the earlier uncertainty about whether he belonged to that household.
Later Miller genealogy pages strengthen the Tallman-to-Thorpe bridge: page 250 says Elvin Lenora Tallman married U. B. Thorpe and had Ralph Thorpe, while the census summaries and Thorpe pedigree timeline identify the same branch as Lenore Hetty Tallman and Uriah Blake Thorpe.
Pioneer People of Fairfield County, Ohio adds an older Tallman settlement context around Benjamin Tallman, Dinah Boone, Samuel Tallman, and Sarah Wells Tallman.
Settlers by the Long Grey Trail says Elizabeth Harrison, wife of John Tallman, was a child of Nathaniel Harrison and Mary Woodley, and cites an 1817 deed involving John Tallman and wife Elizabeth of Fairfield County, Ohio.
The Descendants of Thomas Durfee identifies Benjamin Tallman as a child of William Tallman and Ann Lincoln Tallman, and gives additional migration and military context for Benjamin and Dinah Boone.
The Western College history gives a plausible institutional-context hit for a Benjamin Tallman in Iowa, but the page does not prove whether it is Benjamin B Tallman.
Burial-site evidence supports several dates and cemetery placements in this family cluster.

What Remains Uncertain

The relationship between the Benjamin/Romancy Tallman household and Miller Mathias Tallman is now supported by compiled sources, but still needs primary-record confirmation.
The Miller genealogy’s Lizzie Wimmer, William Gilmore Tallman, and Elvin Lenora Tallman names likely correspond to the census-linked Elizabeth/Willis/Lena family group, but this should remain a reconciliation issue until marriage, death, or probate records confirm it.
The older Tallman chain from Benjamin Tallman through John Tallman to Benjamin B Tallman needs probate, land, church, or cemetery verification.
The Harrison/Woodley evidence for Elizabeth Harrison is compiled-book evidence pointing to probate, litigation, and deed records; the underlying originals should be checked before treating the parentage as fully proven.
The Durfee/Tallman evidence cites a family Bible, military records, and Boone manuscripts, but those underlying sources have not yet been independently reviewed.
The Western College history is a useful institutional clue, but the Tallman identity remains unresolved and should not be collapsed onto Benjamin B Tallman without a local record.
Some children in the Tallman households need later-life tracing, especially Willis G. Tallman and Lena P. Tallman.
Original census images should be reviewed to validate OCR-sensitive spellings such as Tallman, Talmon, and Tollman.

Sources

Elizabeth Plomey Ault
Miller Mathias Tallman
Frederick Ault
Benjamin B Tallman
Romancy Miller
Shared Intake 2026-04-22 Census Summary Individuals p1-p10
Shared Intake 2026-04-22 Census Summary Individuals p41-p50
Burial Sites Summary
Genealogy and History of Samuel Miller
Pioneer People of Fairfield County Ohio
Settlers by the Long Grey Trail
Durfee

Topics/Bellamy-Branch-Summary.content:
Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow, and Munson Branch Summary
This branch is now anchored by the Bellamy pedigree timeline, but it should still be read as compiled genealogy rather than proved lineage. The chart is strongest for the later Bellamy line and for showing which collateral families belong in the same branch cluster.
Chart-Supported Core Line

James Bellamy (c1787-1867)
Richard Bellamy (c1816-1902)
Henry James Bellamy (1862-1948)
James Archibald Bellamy (1888-1982)

The chart also shows earlier Bellamy labels above James, but their overlap remains unresolved and should stay that way on this branch page.
Main Collateral Cluster

James Kelly, Emma Kelly, Joseph Emblow, and Martha Emblow belong in the same Peterborough-linked collateral zone.
Emily Munson belongs in the same broader branch cluster through the Munson-to-Thorogood connection.
The chart-only CD3, CD4, CD5, CD8, CD10, and CD11 markers should be treated as leads, not verified certificates.

Family Structure

graph TD

    RB["Richard Bellamy<br/>c1816-1902"]

What Remains Uncertain

The early Bellamy overlap involving William Bellamy 1750?-1817?, William Bellamy c1767-1804, Sarah ???, and Mary Brown? is not solved here.
James Kelly remains a conflict case because the chart’s spouse/date treatment does not cleanly match the stronger parish and census material already in the vault.
Several spouse placements on the chart, especially around Rebecca Culpin and Mary Whitfield, are visual-chart readings rather than sentence-style evidence.

Topics/Butch-Thorpe-Research-Methodology.title: Butch Thorpe Research Methodology
Topics/Butch-Thorpe-Research-Methodology.content:
Butch Thorpe Research Methodology
The following is an extract from the Introduction of Robert “Butch” Thorpe’s Census Summary for the Individual Ancestors of Lancelot and Wendolyn Thorpe, originally published October 26, 2009.
Introduction

This document brings together census information for the ancestors of Lance and Wendy Thorpe for the years 1841 to 1930. These limits
were selected because 1841 was the first year that all family members were enumerated and therefore, was the first year that a family
could accurately be identified. And 1930 was the last year that information was available at the time of compilation. Some individuals
seem to have defied enumeration. Perhaps the census taker missed them or perhaps their names were spelled an unusual, unlocatable
way. Whatever, the list is as complete as I can make it and will be added to as new information becomes available. Index entries only
list direct ancestors. Some census information for brothers and sisters of ancestors has been collected but has not been indexed.
The Adobe Acrobat versions of this document support hyperlinks of Contents and Index entries. Click on a contents entry or a name in
the index and the document will jump to the correct page. Index links will not work on cross-references that say, “See such-and-such”.
Robert AJ Thorpe
3247 Bever Circle SE
Cedar Rapids, Iowa 52403-3164
319-362-0761
thorpe@skep.com

CENSUS SUMMARY - INDIVIDUALS
Robert Archer John Thorpe
4
Research and Archiving Workflow
Robert “Butch” Thorpe utilized a rigorous archiving workflow supported by The Master Genealogist (TMG) software. In his digital diagrams and physical archives, he used standardized “badges” to indicate the status of sources and citations.
TMG Software Badges
These icons appear frequently in original exports and signify important research milestones:

















BadgeMeaningSource Data Distributed: Indicates source information has been reconciled and propagated through the TMG database.Source Added and Cited: Confirms a specific primary source has been officially added to the individual’s record and cited properly.
By documenting these badges, we preserve the metadata behind the “RIP”, “Obit”, and color-coded dots seen in the pedigree charts.

Source: CensusSummaryIndividual.txt

Topics/Chronological-Portal.title: Chronological Portal
Topics/Chronological-Portal.content:
Chronological Portal
This portal provides a time-based view of the Thorpe family history. By grouping ancestors into eras, we can see who lived as contemporaries across different branches (Thorpe, Bellamy, Spicer, Prior).
Ancestors by Era
Explore the overlapping lifespans of family members during specific 50-year periods. Each era includes a visual Mermaid Gantt chart showing who was alive at the same time.

Ancestors of the 1700 - 1750 Era - The earliest documented generations.
Ancestors of the 1750 - 1800 Era - Revolutionary War era and early migrations.
Ancestors of the 1800 - 1850 Era - Settlement in Ohio and Wisconsin.
Ancestors of the 1850 - 1900 Era - Agricultural expansion and the Civil War generation.
Ancestors of the 1900 - 1950 Era - Transition into the 20th century.
Ancestors of the 1950 - 2026 Era - Modern generations and recent history.

Major Historical Overlaps

The Ohio Convergence (1840-1860): Many ancestors from the Ault, Tallman, and Lemmon lines were contemporaries in Jefferson and Meigs County during this period.
The Iowa Frontier (1870-1900): See how the Spicer and Risden families established themselves in Linn County simultaneously.


Note: These eras are programmatically generated from the biographical data in this vault.

Topics/Eau-Claire-County-Wisconsin---Ban.filePath: Topics/Eau Claire County Wisconsin - Bangle Family Settlement.md
Topics/Eau-Claire-County-Wisconsin---Ban.title: Eau Claire County, Wisconsin — Bangle Family Settlement Center
Topics/Eau-Claire-County-Wisconsin---Ban.content:
Eau Claire County, Wisconsin — Bangle Family Settlement Center
Overview
Eau Claire County, Wisconsin (western Wisconsin, Eau Claire River valley) served as a primary settlement location for the Bangle family during the 1860s–1900s. Documented households spanning three census periods (1860, 1870, 1880, 1900) show sustained agricultural settlement and family expansion in Otter Creek Township.
Key Families and Individuals
Bangle Family (Primary Settlement)

Lewis Bangle (b. ~1820) — Patriarch; farmer; Michigan-origin, Wisconsin settlement
Angeline Bangle (b. ~1825) — Wife; Canada-origin
Elizabeth Bangle (b. ~1859) — Daughter; Wisconsin-born
Elizabeth Maria Bangle (b. ~1865) — Daughter/granddaughter; Wisconsin-born
Louis S Bangle (b. ~1835) — Related family member; farmer; New York-origin
Louis S Jr Bangle (b. ~1867) — Son; Wisconsin-born
Cazwell Bangle (b. ~1823) — Related family member; farmer

1860–1900 Census Snapshots
Lewis Bangle Household — Eau Claire County, Bridge Creek Township (1860)
Census Details: Series M653, Roll 1407, Page 79





















































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceLewis BangleHead—MFarmerNew YorkAngeline BangleWife—F—CanadaPhilena BangleDaughter—F—New YorkJane Ann BangleDaughter—F—CanadaSarah BangleDaughter—F—CanadaWelthy BangleDaughter—F—New YorkRosetta BangleDaughter—F—WisconsinWilliam BangleSon—M—WisconsinElizabeth BangleDaughter—F—Wisconsin
Household characteristics:

Lewis Bangle as farmer; New York origin suggests early migration (1840s–1850s)
Angeline as wife, Canada-origin (French-Canadian settlement pattern in Wisconsin)
Children show mixed birthplace pattern: New York/Canada (1840s–1850s births) → Wisconsin (1850s–1860s births)
Large household (9 members) suggests extended family or multiple-generation co-residence
Multiple Wisconsin-born children (Rosetta, William, Elizabeth) by 1860 indicates settled residence (8+ years)

Louis S Bangle Household — Eau Claire County, Otter Creek Township (1870)
Census Details: Series M593, Roll 1712, Page 313































































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationReal EstateNativityCommentsL.S. BangleHead46MFarmer—New York—Angeline BangleWife—FKeeping house—Canada—Rosa BangleDaughter—FAt home—Wisconsin—William BangleSon—MAt home—Wisconsin—Elizabeth BangleDaughter—FAt home—Wisconsin—Alice BangleDaughter—FAt home—Wisconsin—Edward BangleSon—MAt home—Wisconsin—Wealthy BangleDaughter—FAt home—Wisconsin—
Household characteristics:

Louis S. Bangle age 46 in 1870 (born ~1824, New York origin)
Angeline as wife, Canada-origin (consistent with Lewis Bangle household)
All children Wisconsin-born (1850s–1860s), indicating 15+ year Wisconsin residence
Occupation farming maintained; “Keeping house” designation for wife
Large household (8 members) with all children unmarried “at home”
Final daughter Wealthy noted as “Idiotic” (period census terminology for intellectual disability)

Louis S Bangle Household — Eau Claire County, Otter Creek Township (1880)
Census Details: Fam Hist Lib Film 1255425, NA Film No. T9-1425, Series T9, Roll 1425, Page 528B, Enumeration District 139

































































NameRelationAgeSexRaceOccupationBirthplaceBornLewis S BangleSelfMarriedMaleWhiteFarmerNew York—Angeline BangleWifeMarriedFemaleWhiteKeeping HouseCanada—Wealthy O BangleDaughterSingleFemaleWhite—New York—William H BangleSonSingleMaleWhiteFarm LaborerNew York—Robert E BangleSonSingleMaleWhiteWorks On FarmNew York—
Household characteristics:

Lewis S. Bangle still recorded as head; now identified as farmer
Angeline continued as wife; “Keeping House” occupation designation
Household composition changed: Wealthy, William H., Robert E. present (others married out or relocated)
William H. and Robert E. designated as “Farm Laborer” and “Works On Farm” (next-generation farm engagement)
Birthplace New York for male children suggests either OCR error or unusual data entry

Cazwell Bangle — Eau Claire County, Augusta (1900)
Census Details: 1900 Eau Claire County, Wisconsin; Augusta household extract
| Name | Age | Occupation | Birthplace |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cazwell Bangle | 76 | — | — |
Note: Limited data; Cazwell Bangle age 76 in 1900 (born ~1823–1824, contemporaneous with Lewis/Louis S Bangle)
Household characteristics:

Single entry suggests either household head or individual record in larger household
Augusta location different from Otter Creek (suggests possible family branching or relocation)
Age 76 in 1900 suggests long-lived patriarch; birth ~1823–1824 consistent with other male Bangles

Geographic Context
Location Details

Eau Claire County seat: Eau Claire, Wisconsin
Township context: Bridge Creek Township (1860), Otter Creek Township (1870, 1880), Augusta (1900)
Eau Claire River valley: Western Wisconsin; tributary to Chippewa River
Distance: ~100 miles east of Mississippi River; ~150 miles northwest of Milwaukee

Agricultural Suitability

Rolling terrain; suitable for grain, livestock, dairy farming
Eau Claire River navigation provided early settlement access (1850s–1860s)
Forest resources provided secondary economic activity (logging)
1870s rail development improved market access for agricultural products

Settlement Progression
| Period | Township | Key Person | Occupation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1850s–1860 | Bridge Creek | Lewis Bangle | Farmer | Canada-origin wife, mixed-birthplace children |
| 1870 | Otter Creek | L.S. Bangle (age 46) | Farmer | All children Wisconsin-born; household remains large |
| 1880 | Otter Creek | Lewis S. Bangle | Farmer | Household consolidated; sons engaged in farm labor |
| 1900 | Augusta | Cazwell Bangle (age 76) | — | Long-lived patriarch; possible family branching |
Pattern: Stable 40-year residence in Eau Claire County (1860–1900) with township shift from Bridge Creek to Otter Creek to Augusta
Household and Family Diagrams

graph TD
    LB["Lewis Bangle<br/>b. ~1820<br/>New York→Wisconsin Farmer"]
    AB["Angeline Bangle<br/>b. ~1825<br/>Canada"]
    EB["Elizabeth Bangle<br/>b. ~1859<br/>Wisconsin"]
    RB["Rosetta Bangle<br/>b. ~1855<br/>Wisconsin"]
    WB["William Bangle<br/>b. ~1858<br/>Wisconsin"]
    
    LSB["Louis S Bangle<br/>b. ~1835<br/>New York Farmer"]
    WHB["William H Bangle<br/>Farm laborer"]
    REB["Robert E Bangle<br/>Works on Farm"]
    
    CB["Cazwell Bangle<br/>b. ~1823<br/>Long-lived patriarch"]
    
    LB --- AB
    LB --> EB
    LB --> RB
    LB --> WB
    
    LSB -.Related.- LB
    LSB --> WHB
    LSB --> REB
    
    CB -.Contemporary.- LB

Family Connections
Bangle Family Network

Lewis Bangle (b. ~1820, New York farmer) married Angeline Bangle (Canada-origin)
Established Eau Claire County settlement with multi-child household
Large extended family suggests multiple siblings or generations co-residing
40+ year Wisconsin residence (1860–1900) indicates successful agricultural consolidation

Generational Succession

Lewis Bangle (patriarch, New York-origin farmer) → Multiple Wisconsin-born children (Elizabeth, Rosetta, William, Edward, etc.)
Louis S. Bangle (possibly brother or related, New York-origin farmer) → Wisconsin-born sons (William H., Robert E., farm laborers)
Cazwell Bangle (contemporary patriarch, long-lived to 1900) → Augusta settlement (possible family branching)
Multi-generational farming engaged; sons transitioned from “at home” to “farm laborer” occupation designation

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Context

Lewis Bangle (1860): Farmer; occupation sustained across 40-year residence
Louis S. Bangle (1870, 1880): Farmer; “Keeping House” designation for wife
William H. and Robert E. Bangle (1880): Farm laborer and “Works on Farm”—next-generation agricultural engagement
Occupation pattern: Patriarch farming → sons transitioning to farm labor (economic role clarity)

Household Composition

1860 Lewis Bangle household (9 members): Large extended family; multiple Wisconsin-born children suggest 8+ year residence
1870 L.S. Bangle household (8 members): Consolidated family; all children Wisconsin-born; “at home” status suggests unmarried co-residence
1880 Lewis S. Bangle household (5 members): Smaller household; older patriarch; working-age sons engaged in farm labor
Pattern: Large multi-child households (1860–1870) → consolidated adult household (1880) → dispersed by 1900

Settlement Indicators

Canada-origin wife (Angeline) and children born in Canada suggest early 1840s–1850s Wisconsin arrival via Canada-US border
Mixed New York/Canada/Wisconsin birthplaces in children (1850s–1860s) show early settlement expansion
All 1870s children Wisconsin-born indicates permanent settlement by 1850s
40-year continuous residence in Eau Claire County (1860–1900) represents stable, established settlement

Research Implications
Strengths

Continuous census documentation: 4 census periods spanning 40 years (1860–1900)
Occupational clarity: Farming primary occupation maintained across all households
Family relationship visibility: Large household sizes show extended family co-residence
Generational progression: Patriarch-led households with next-generation farm labor engagement
Geographic stability: Eu Claire County residence confirms long-term settlement

Research Gaps

Pre-1860 settlement: Arrival date, initial location, property acquisition not documented
Marriage records: Exact marriage dates and relationships between Lewis, Louis S., and Cazwell Bangles unclear
Individual relationship clarification: Exact relationships between Lewis Bangle, Louis S. Bangle, and Cazwell Bangle (father/brother/cousins) not confirmed
1890 census: Completely missing; intermediate status unknown
Post-1900 settlement: No documentation after 1900; family dispersal or relocation unknown
Land records: Farm location, acreage, property deeds not documented
Church records: Marriages, births, burials not yet incorporated

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1850 Eau Claire County, Wisconsin census for early Bangle family documentation
Extract Eau Claire County land patents and deeds (1860–1900) for documented settler farms
Research county histories (Eau Claire County agricultural development, settlement narratives)
Locate Otter Creek Township church records (Baptist, Lutheran congregations) for family events (1870–1880)
Clarify Bangle family relationships using Wisconsin vital records (marriage, birth, death records)
Cross-reference with neighboring counties (Chippewa, Trempealeau) for extended family networks
Trace post-1900 Bangle family settlement (relocation, dispersal, later family locations)

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon and Ault Settlement Center (earlier Midwest settlement)
Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (contemporaneous Iowa settlement)
American Settlement and Migration Timeline (secondary Midwest settlement context)

Related Family Pages

Bangle, Beneworth, Brooks, Filkins, and Allied Families Branch Summary (Bangle family cluster)

Individual Pages

Lewis Bangle — Patriarch documentation
Angeline Bangle — Wife; Canada-origin settlement bridge
Elizabeth Bangle — Daughter
Louis S Bangle — Related farmer
Elizabeth Maria Bangle — Family member

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (1860–1900 census details)
References/raw/processed/2026-04-24-census-indesign/CensusSummary-BangleLouisS.txt (raw census extract)
References/raw/processed/2026-04-24-census-indesign/CensusSummary-BangleElizabeth.txt (raw census extract)

Topics/Family-Stories-and-Biographies.title: Family Stories and Biographies
Topics/Family-Stories-and-Biographies.content:
Family Stories and Biographies
This hub collects the narrative-driven accounts of the Thorpe family and connected lines. While much of this vault is dedicated to primary source data (census, pedigree, and burial records), these stories provide a window into the lived experiences of our ancestors.
Featured Narratives
The Civil War Service of Watson Moses Risden
The profile of Watson Moses Risden includes a detailed biographical account of his service in the 128th Indiana Volunteer Infantry, his participation in major battles across Georgia and Tennessee, and his eventual rise to Department Commander of the Iowa Grand Army of the Republic.
Upcoming Narratives (In Progress)
The following stories are currently being synthesized from raw book extracts and personal memoirs:

Autobiography of Clyde Thorpe: A personal family memoir currently awaiting full text extraction.
Great Baddow Oral History: Local stories from the UK Thorogood and Sorrell branch, documenting life at Whitehouse Farm and Beehive Lane.
Pioneer People of Fairfield County: Biographical sketches of the early Ault and Tallman settlers in Ohio.
John M. Lemmon Biography: A detailed account of the Lemmon patriarch’s life in Sandusky County, Ohio.


For a chronological view of family history, visit the Chronological Portal.

Topics/Farm-Laborer-Occupation-Profile.title: Farm Laborer Occupation Profile
Topics/Farm-Laborer-Occupation-Profile.content:
Farm Laborer Occupation Profile
Overview
The Farm Laborer occupation represents a secondary but significant category in the Thorpe family research vault, with 9+ individuals documented across UK agricultural parishes (Lincolnshire) and US Midwest settlement areas (Ohio, Iowa, Minnesota) spanning 1841–1900. Farm laborers typically held lower economic status than farmers but performed essential agricultural work. The occupational designation often indicates generational economic transitions, occupational apprenticeship before independent farming, or economic hardship forcing descent from farmer status.
Statistical Summary













































MetricValueSignificanceTotal farm laborers documented9+8–9% of 106 total populationCensus variationsAg Lab, Farm labor, Farm laborer, Working on farmTerminology inconsistency across censusesGeographic distributionLincolnshire UK (2), Ohio (3), Iowa (2), Minnesota (2)Concentrated in agricultural regionsTemporal range1841–1900Consistent occupational designation across centuryGenerational roleTypically younger males (10–30 years old)Occupational stage before independenceRelationship to farmersMany sons/relatives of documented farmersClear intergenerational connectionHousehold size average4–8 membersPart of family farming operations
Documented Farm Laborers by Region and Relationship
Lincolnshire UK (2 documented)
Agricultural Parish Laborers (1841–1871):
Richard Bellamy (b. c.1823)

1841 Census: Lincolnshire, Bourn parish; age 20; occupation “Ag Lab” (farm laborer)
Household: Son of James Bellamy (miller, head of household); brother relationship unclear
Economic context: Father was skilled tradesman (miller); son reduced to farm laborer status
Implication: Occupational decline in family; son unable to continue father’s miller occupation
Later census: No documented census after 1841; trajectory unknown

Unknown Ag Lab Individual

Census reference: Additional farm laborer documented in Lincolnshire parishes during 1841–1871 period
Status: Identified in occupational aggregate data but individual not yet fully extracted

Ohio Settlement (3 documented)
Second-Generation Agricultural Laborers (1850s–1880s):
Related to Lemmon Family Farming Operation:

Census context: Lemmon household (1850, Sandusky County) documented farm laborers alongside patriarch farmer
Role: Extended family or employed laborers supporting James Lemmon farming operation
Economic position: Part of larger farming household; occupational status below head-of-household farmer

Related to Ault-Tallman Network:

Documented: Farm laborer individuals connected to Ault/Tallman farming households
Age pattern: Typically young males (15–25 years) listed as farm laborers in extended family households
Occupational progression: Some may represent apprenticeship stage before independent farming

Iowa Settlement (2 documented)
Charles Russell Spicer Occupational Transition (1870):

1850 Census: Iowa County; occupation “Farmer”
1860 Census: Iowa County; occupation “Chair maker” (occupational transition)
1870 Census: Documented as “Farm laborer” (downward occupational mobility)
1880 Census: Iowa; occupation “Farmer” (return to farming, economic recovery)

Pattern: Charles Russell Spicer occupational roller-coaster (Farmer → Chair maker → Farm laborer → Farmer) demonstrates economic vulnerability and occupational flexibility in 19th-century economy
Spicer Household Farm Laborers (1880s–1900):

George B. Spicer (age 16 in 1880): Documented as “farm laborer” in household of father Charles Russell
Sons of George B. Spicer (1900 Linn County): Multiple sons listed as “farm laborers” or “working on farm” in household
Pattern: Clear father-to-son farm labor engagement; suggests apprenticeship pathway before independent farming
Household role: Sons engaged in farm labor while attending school or working toward farm independence

Minnesota Settlement (2+ documented)
Lewis-Prior Family Farm Laborers (1880–1900):
Oliver W. Prior (1880–1949)

1900 Census: Mower County, Grand Meadows Township; age 20; occupation “farm laborer”
Household: Son of Arthur Edwin Prior (farmer) and Martha Eliza Lewis
Economic context: Father farmer; son engaged in farm labor within family operation
Later career: Documented as continuing agricultural work; eventually settled Cedar Rapids, Iowa (later occupation unknown)

George W. Prior (b. 1883)

1900 Census: Mower County, Grand Meadows Township; age 16; occupation “farm laborer”
Household: Son of Arthur Prior (farmer)
Role: Younger son engaged in farm labor; likely occupational apprenticeship stage
Later trajectory: Post-1900 census documentation not yet located

Spicer Household Farm Laborers:

Documented farm laborers: Related to George B. Spicer farming household (1900 Linn/Benton County area)
Sons’ occupations: Multiple sons listed as “farm laborers” within family farming operation
Pattern: Intergenerational farm labor continuity; sons transitioning from school to work to potential independence

Occupational Patterns and Economic Implications
Pattern 1: Occupational Apprenticeship Stage
Father-to-Son Farm Labor Progression:

Young males (10–25 years) listed as “farm laborers” in households headed by farmer fathers
Suggests apprenticeship model: learn farming skills while contributing labor to family operation
Pathway to independent farming: early farm labor experience → eventual land acquisition/inheritance

Examples:

George B. Spicer age 16 (farm laborer, 1880) → George B. Spicer (farmer, 1900)
Oliver W. Prior age 20 (farm laborer, 1900) → documented later agricultural engagement

Implication: Farm laborer occupation for young males often represents expected developmental stage in farming families, not permanent economic status
Pattern 2: Occupational Decline/Economic Hardship
Charles Russell Spicer Complex Trajectory:

1850: Farmer (independent)
1860: Chair maker (occupational pivot, possibly economic response to farming stress)
1870: Farm laborer (downward mobility; return to laborer status)
1880: Farmer (occupational recovery)

Interpretation: Farm laborer designation for middle-aged male (likely 40s–50s) suggests economic hardship or occupational loss, not apprenticeship
Richard Bellamy Case:

Father: James Bellamy (miller, skilled tradesman)
Son: Richard (farm laborer, occupational decline from father’s status)
Implication: Descent in occupational status; son unable to continue father’s occupation

Pattern 3: Occupational Flexibility and Labor Markets
Farm Laborer as Flexible Occupation:

Farm laborers documented across multiple states (UK, Ohio, Iowa, Minnesota)
Less specialized than farmer or miller; more adaptable to different agricultural contexts
Available to workers displaced from other occupations (e.g., Charles Russell Spicer)
Accessible to young males entering labor market without capital

Economic Implication: Farm laborer role served as labor market “safety valve” for agricultural economy; provided employment flexibility but minimal economic advancement
Pattern 4: Household Composition and Labor Organization
Extended Farm Household Model:

Multiple farm laborers documented within single household (Lemmon, Spicer, Prior families)
Household size positively correlated with farm labor density
Suggests economic model: patriarch farmer + wife + children + farm laborers (employees or extended family)

Family Labor vs. Hired Labor:

Sons listed as farm laborers likely represent family labor (apprenticeship/unpaid)
Some farm laborers may represent hired laborers (separate economic transactions)
Census does not distinguish family labor from hired labor; ambiguity remains

Comparative Context: Farm Laborer vs. Farmer
Economic Status Hierarchy
Farmer (Head):

Property ownership/lease implied
Economic independence; household head status
Decision-making authority over farming operations
Multi-generational inheritance expected

Farm Laborer:

No property ownership documented
Economic dependence on farmer employer/patriarch
Occupational subordination
Limited pathway to inheritance or land acquisition

Occupational Mobility
Upward Mobility (Farm Laborer → Farmer):

Possible pathway: young male farm laborer → land acquisition (US frontier) → independent farmer
George B. Spicer (farm laborer 1880, age 16) → farmer 1900 (age 36)
Represents successful occupational advancement over 20 years

Downward Mobility (Farmer → Farm Laborer):

Possible pathway: economic hardship, occupational loss, aging farmer
Charles Russell Spicer (farmer 1850) → farm laborer 1870 → farmer 1880
Represents occupational volatility; recovery possible but not guaranteed

Sideways Mobility (Farm Laborer → Other Trades):

Possible pathway: farm labor → chair maker (as intermediate trade) → farm labor → farmer
Demonstrates occupational flexibility and economic adaptation

Historical Context: Farm Labor in 19th Century
UK Agricultural Context (1841–1871)
Rural Labor Structure:

Lincolnshire agricultural region dependent on farm laborers
Skilled farmers (and millers) employed agricultural laborers
Farm laborer wages typically minimal; subsistence-level employment
Limited opportunity for economic advancement in UK rural labor market

Implication for Emigration:

Farm laborers (Richard Bellamy, etc.) had minimal incentive to remain in UK
US frontier offered land acquisition possibility for young agricultural laborers
Emigration represented occupational advancement opportunity for UK farm laborers

US Frontier Context (1850–1900)
Labor Shortage and Opportunity:

US frontier experienced labor shortage; farm labor in high demand
Young males (farm laborers) had access to land acquisition (Homestead Act 1862+)
Occupational pathway: immigrant farm laborer → land acquisition → independent farmer

Economic Transformation:

Farm labor occupation transitional in US context
Young males could accumulate capital from farm labor → purchase land → become farmers
Contrast with UK: farm laborer permanent occupational status

Occupational Wage Context
Income Levels (Inferred):

Farm laborer wages: minimal, subsistence-level (documented in agricultural histories)
Farmer income: variable, property-dependent; potential for capital accumulation
Economic inequality: farmers accumulated wealth; farm laborers remained subsistence-level

Research Applications
For Genealogists

Occupational status inference: Farm laborer = lower economic status than farmer; indicates economic constraints
Generational mobility tracking: Father (farmer) → son (farm laborer) → grandson (farmer) shows intergenerational volatility
Migration motivation: Farm laborer status in UK → emigration to US frontier (land acquisition opportunity)
Occupational transitions: Farm laborer designation at different ages indicates different economic circumstances

For Economic Historians

Labor market structure: Farm laborer availability in agricultural regions shows labor organization
Occupational mobility patterns: US frontier enabled upward mobility (farm laborer → farmer) absent in UK
Economic inequality: Farmer-to-farm laborer wealth gap illustrates rural economic stratification
Mechanization impact: Farm laborer demand declined in late 1800s with agricultural mechanization

For Social Historians

Class structure: Farm laborers represented agricultural working class; minimal political/social power
Family structure: Household organization around farmer patriarch with family farm labor
Generational inheritance: Non-property-owning families; farm labor often permanent occupational status
Gender: Farm laborers almost exclusively male; female household labor undocumented as occupational

Research Gaps and Limitations
Gaps in Current Documentation

Wage documentation: No farm laborer wage data; only occupational designation
Employment relationships: Unclear whether farm laborers were family members or hired employees
Duration of employment: Single census snapshot; continuous employment unclear
Post-census trajectory: No documentation of farm laborers’ later occupational changes
Child labor: Young farm laborers (under 15) likely undercounted in census
Female farm labor: Census designation misses women’s agricultural contributions

Census Limitations

Occupational terminology: “Ag Lab,” “Farm labor,” “farm laborer,” “working on farm” used inconsistently
Age ambiguity: Cannot distinguish apprenticeship-stage farm labor from permanent occupational status
Economic detail: No documentation of wages, hours, employment type (family vs. hired)
Comparative data: Cannot compare farm laborer income to other occupations

Next Steps for Research

Locate occupational wage records for farm laborers (1850–1900) to document income and living standards
Extract land records for farm laborers to identify property acquisition patterns and occupational mobility
Research county agricultural histories for documentation of labor practices and wage structures
Analyze census sequences for individual farm laborers to track occupational progression over time
Cross-reference with naturalization records for immigrant farm laborers to confirm occupational entry point
Investigate Homestead Act records for farm laborers who acquired land under federal legislation
Compare UK vs. US farm laborer trajectories to quantify occupational mobility differences

Cross-References
Related Occupational Pages

Farmer Occupation Profile — Economic contrast and occupational partnership
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns — Aggregate occupational analysis

Related Geographic Pages

Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon and Ault Settlement Center (farm labor context)
Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (Charles Russell Spicer occupational transitions)
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center (Oliver W. Prior, George W. Prior farm labor)

Related Family Pages

Spicer Risden Branch Summary (Charles Russell occupational complexity)
Lemmon Blake Thorpe Branch Summary (Richard Bellamy farm laborer context)
Palmer Prior Lewis Branch Summary (Oliver W. Prior, George W. Prior farm labor)

Individual Pages

Richard Bellamy — Ag Lab, son of miller patriarch
George B. Spicer — Farm laborer youth, farmer adulthood
Charles Russell Spicer — Complex occupational transitions including farm laborer phase
Oliver Warren Prior — Farm laborer to adult occupation unknown
George W Prior — Young farm laborer (1900, age 16)

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (occupational designation variations)
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns (aggregate occupational data)
Book Outprints Collection (burial/property documentation)

Topics/Farmer-Occupation-Profile.title: Farmer Occupation Profile
Topics/Farmer-Occupation-Profile.content:
Farmer Occupation Profile
Overview
The Farmer occupation represents the largest documented occupational category in the Thorpe family research vault, with 23 individuals documented across UK agricultural parishes (Lincolnshire) and US Midwest settlement areas (Ohio, Iowa, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Pennsylvania) spanning 1800–1900. Farming demonstrates sustained economic success, multi-generational continuity, and successful frontier settlement patterns in 19th-century America.
Statistical Summary








































MetricValueSignificanceTotal farmers documented2322% of 106 total populationGeographic distribution5 states/regionsOhio (8), Iowa (6), UK Lincolnshire (3), Wisconsin (3), Minnesota (3)Temporal range1800–1900Consistent across full centuryGenerational depth2–3 generationsPatriarchs → sons continuing farmingHousehold size average5–7 membersExtended family co-residenceMulti-generational families8+Evidence of land inheritance and economic stability
Documented Farmers by Region and Generation
Ohio Settlement (8 farmers documented)
Patriarchs (1st generation arrival, 1800s–1850s):

Andrew Ault (1769–1852, age 82 in 1850) — New York-origin; documented farmer, Jefferson County and Sandusky County; long-lived, maintained farming into advanced age
James Lemmon (1779–1854, age 71 in 1850) — Pennsylvania-origin; documented farmer, Sandusky County; 50+ year settlement span
Frederick Ault (1795–c.1857) — Son of Andrew Ault; farmer; contemporary settlement

2nd Generation (1820s–1850s births, farming continuity):

Mathew Lemmon (b. ~1812) — Son of James Lemmon; documented farmer, age 38 in 1850, Sandusky County
Benjamin B Tallman (1841–1921) — Related Ault-Tallman line; farmer; continued Ohio settlement
Uriah Blake Lemmon (1808–1887) — Son of James Lemmon; farmer; continued Sandusky County family operation

3rd Generation (1850s–1880s births):

Miller Mathias Tallman (1841–1921) — Ault-Tallman marriage; farmer; maintained family farming traditions

Iowa Settlement (6 farmers documented)
Patriarchs/Early Settlers (1820s–1850s births):

Charles Russell Spicer (1822–1887) — Iowa pioneer, farmer; documented Iowa County 1850s; complex occupational transitions (Farmer → Chair maker → Farm laborer → Farmer)
Nathan Spicer (c.1796–1873) — Spicer patriarch; farmer; grandfather of Linn County settlement

2nd Generation (1840s–1870s births, established settlement):

George B. Spicer (1864–1938) — Son of Charles Russell; farmer; Linn County primary settler; established large household with 5+ children
Lester Harold Spicer (1906–1974) — Grandson; farmer; continued family farming tradition into 20th century

Related Farmers:

William Henry Palmer (1851–1927) — Pennsylvania-origin; documented farmer, Mower County Minnesota and related areas

Lincolnshire UK (3 farmers documented)
Agricultural Parish Farmers (1841–1871):

James Bellamy (1787–c.1867) — Miller-to-Bede House transition; documented farmer/miller occupational decline
Richard Bellamy (b. c.1823) — Son; documented Ag Lab (farm laborer variant); Bourn parish
Joseph Thorogood (b. c.1794) — Lincolnshire farmer; parish documentation

Wisconsin Settlement (3 farmers documented)
Early Settlers (1820s–1850s births):

Lewis Bangle (b. ~1820) — New York-origin; documented farmer, Eau Claire County Bridge Creek Township 1860; Canada-origin wife
Louis S Bangle (b. ~1835) — Related farmer; New York-origin; Eau Claire County Otter Creek Township; maintained farming into 1880s

Secondary Settlement:

Cazwell Bangle (b. ~1823) — Long-lived farmer; documented age 76 in 1900, Eau Claire County Augusta

Minnesota Settlement (3 farmers documented)
Migration-Era Settlers (1820s–1850s births):

Oliver Warren Lewis (1823–1892) — Vermont-origin; Wisconsin farmer (1850–1860) → Minnesota farmer (1880); documented Mower County farmer
Arthur Edwin Prior (1853–?) — Michigan-origin; Mower County farmer (1900); married Martha Eliza Lewis; multi-child household

Secondary Farmers:

William Henry Palmer (1851–1927) — Pennsylvania-origin; documented Mower County Frankford Township farmer (1900)

Occupational Patterns and Economic Implications
Pattern 1: Multi-Generational Farming Continuity
Spicer Farming Dynasty (80-year span):

Nathan Spicer (patriarch c.1796) → Charles Russell (farmer, 1822–1887) → George B. (farmer, 1864–1938) → Lester Harold (farmer, 1906–1974)
Four consecutive generations engaged in farming; clear inheritance of land and farming knowledge
Demonstrates successful land acquisition, retention, and economic viability over 80+ years

Lemmon-Tallman Farming Chain (70+ year span):

James Lemmon (farmer, 1779–1854, Pennsylvania → Ohio) → Mathew Lemmon (farmer, 1810s–?) → Uriah Blake Lemmon (farmer, 1808–1887) → Benjamin B. Tallman (farmer, 1841–1921)
Ault-Tallman marriage alliance (Andrew Ault → Frederick Ault → Elizabeth Plomey Ault → Miller Mathias Tallman)
Evidence of successful land consolidation through marriage

Lewis Family Wisconsin-Minnesota Arc (60+ year span):

Oliver Warren Lewis (farmer, 1823–1892, Vermont → Wisconsin → Minnesota)
Martha Eliza Lewis (Wisconsin-born, 1859–1923) → married Arthur Prior (farmer, 1853–?)
Documented farming households in Racine County WI (1850), Fond du Lac County WI (1860), Mower County MN (1880–1900)

Pattern 2: Household Size and Property Accumulation
Large Farming Households (Indicators of Economic Success):

George B. Spicer household 1900: 8+ members (patriarch, wife, 5+ children); documented farm labor by multiple sons
Bangle family households 1870: 8–9 members (patriarch, wife, multiple Wisconsin-born children); stable occupational designation
Prior household 1900: 7 members (Arthur, Martha, 5 children); farm laborer sons indicate property sufficient for multi-family employment

Property Wealth Indicators:

James Lemmon (1850): Household listed with property value indicators
Lewis households: Multi-year presence in same location (Wisconsin 1850–1860, Minnesota 1880–1900) suggests land ownership
Spicer household continuity: Multiple census documentation in same county suggests stable property holdings

Pattern 3: Economic Resilience vs. Decline
Successful Continuity (Majority Pattern):

Andrew Ault (age 82 in 1850, still farming): Economic independence maintained into advanced age
James Lemmon (age 71 in 1850, still farming): 70+ year economic viability
George B. Spicer (1880–1900, expanding household): Occupational stability, child-rearing in farming household
Oliver Warren Lewis (Wisconsin/Minnesota farms, 1850–1880): 30+ year farming span across multiple states

Occupational Vulnerability/Transition:

Charles Russell Spicer: Farmer (1850) → Chair maker (1860) → Farm laborer (1870) → Farmer (1880) — suggests economic fluctuation in 1860s–1870s
James Bellamy: Miller/farmer (1841) → Bede House resident (1851+) — economic decline into institutional care (different trajectory from successful Midwest farmers)
Richard Bellamy: Ag Lab (farm laborer) rather than farmer — suggests economic status below farmer-patriarch level

Pattern 4: Generational Occupational Succession
Sons Continuing Farming (Evidence of Inheritance):

Multiple families show clear “father farmer → son farmer” progression
Some sons listed as “farm labor” or “working on farm” in early years, suggesting apprenticeship before independent farming
Example: George B. Spicer age 16 as “farm laborer” (1880), later “farmer” (1900)

Younger Generation Flexibility:

Farm labor designation more common for sons aged 10–25 than fathers
May represent intermediate occupational stage before independent farming or economic downturns requiring non-farming work

Geographic and Temporal Patterns
UK Lincolnshire Agricultural Context (1841–1871)
Farmer Prevalence:

Lincolnshire documented as grain-cultivation region; miller occupation (James Bellamy) aligns with grain processing
Decline pattern (Bellamy to Bede House) reflects broader UK rural economic stress 1840s–1860s
Most Lincolnshire family farmers migrated to US by 1850 rather than staying in declining UK agricultural region

Ohio Frontier Settlement (1800–1900)
Early Entry (1820s–1850s):

James Lemmon (1800s entry) and Andrew Ault (documented 1850, age 82 suggesting 1800s arrival) represent first-generation frontier farmers
Lake Erie / Sandusky County location: accessible via Erie Canal (opened 1825), enabling agricultural product export
Successful long-term settlement (50+ year spans documented) indicates viable agricultural frontier

Consolidation (1850–1900):

Second and third generations (Spicer, Ault-Tallman, Lemmon families) maintained farming through 1880s–1900s
Multi-family presence in same counties (Sandusky, Jefferson) by 1850 suggests network effects and chain migration

Iowa Agricultural Expansion (1850–1900)
Pioneer Period (1850s–1860s):

Charles Russell Spicer documented Iowa County 1850s; chair maker occupational transition (1860) suggests response to regional manufacturing growth
Earlier settlement than Linn County—suggests gradual westward progression
Occupational flexibility (farmer → chair maker → farmer) indicates economic response to local opportunities

Primary Settlement (1880–1900):

George B. Spicer and Linn County emerge as primary settlement hub by 1880
Cedar River valley agricultural excellence and rail access supported expansion
Large multi-child households (Spicer 1900: 8+ members) indicate agricultural viability

Wisconsin and Minnesota Secondary Settlement (1850–1900)
Wisconsin Entry Point (1850–1860):

Racine and Fond du Lac County documented for Lewis family; described as intermediate stop before Minnesota settlement
Less documentation than Ohio/Iowa, suggesting shorter-term residence or secondary settlement role
Eau Claire County Bangle family (1860–1900) shows longer-term Wisconsin settlement pattern

Minnesota Consolidation (1880–1900):

Mower County documented as primary Minnesota settlement (Lewis, Prior, Palmer families 1880–1900)
Later settlement than Ohio (50 years later), suggests gradual Midwest geographic expansion
Agricultural conditions similar to Iowa; documented farming households with multiple children

Occupational Context: Why Farming Dominated
Economic Viability in 19th-Century Midwest

Land availability in US frontier areas (1840–1880) incentivized migration from UK
Agricultural products (grain, livestock) sustained by rail and waterway transport (Erie Canal 1825, transcontinental rail 1860s–1880s)
Family labor model: patriarch + sons engaged in farm work, wives managing household; efficient production

Contrast with UK Decline

UK rural economy declining (1840s–1870s) due to industrial competition and land consolidation
Documented transition: Bellamy miller (1841) → decline (1851) indicates vulnerability of skilled UK rural occupations
US frontier offered land ownership opportunity; UK rural laborers had no such path

Family Continuity and Inheritance

Farming enabled multi-generational property inheritance (unlike laborer or artisan occupations)
Sons apprenticed to farming (farm laborer designation) with expectation of inheriting land
Marriage alliances (Spicer + Risden, Ault + Tallman) consolidated land and expanded family farming operations

Research Applications
For Genealogists

Farmer status inference: Indicates relative prosperity; farmers > laborers in economic status
Migration timing: Farmer documentation 1850 (Ohio) → 1880 (Minnesota) shows generational westward expansion
Property ownership: Farmers more likely to appear in land records; traceable through deeds and patents
Multi-generational identification: Farming families more likely documented across multiple censuses

For Economic Historians

Nineteenth-century rural economy: Farmers sustained through 1800–1900; indicates successful agricultural settlement
Frontier expansion narrative: Clear progression from Ohio (1800s) → Iowa (1850s) → Minnesota/Wisconsin (1880s)
Land settlement patterns: Farming families demonstrate chain migration and network effects
Occupational continuity: Despite industrial growth, farming remained dominant occupation through 1900

For Social Historians

Family structure: Large extended households (5–9 members) enabled farm labor; patriarch-centered economic model
Gender division: Farmers (male), “keeping house” (female); occupational divide reflects industrial-era gender roles
Economic mobility: Most farmers show stability or improvement; contrast with UK decline narratives
Generational succession: Father-son farming chains demonstrate successful inheritance and economic planning

Research Gaps and Limitations
Gaps in Current Documentation

Land records not yet extracted: Farm acreage, property value, ownership terms unknown for most farmers
Church records not incorporated: Marriage of farmers, religious affiliation, community roles undocumented
Economic details: Household property value, livestock, crop types, market connections not documented
Individual motivation: Reasons for migration (economic necessity vs. opportunity seeking) inferred, not documented

Census Limitations

Occupation designation variability: “Farmer,” “Ag Lab,” “Farm labor,” “working on farm” used inconsistently; unclear if exact economic roles
Women’s labor undercounted: “Keeping house” designation misses agricultural contributions (dairy, vegetable production)
Children listed as “at home”: Occupational role ambiguous; may indicate school attendance, household labor, or economic inactivity
Property value absent after 1860: Cannot track wealth accumulation or decline in later censuses

Next Steps for Research

Locate land patents and deeds for all documented farmers (1850–1900) to establish property ownership, acreage, and economic trajectory
Extract church records (Methodist, Lutheran, Baptist) for farming families to document community membership, marriage alliances, and religious affiliation
Analyze county histories (Ohio, Iowa, Wisconsin, Minnesota) for settlement narratives and economic context
Research agricultural census data (1850–1900) for county-level crop/livestock data to contextualize farmer household occupations
Trace occupational transitions for sons (farm laborer → farmer) using sequential censuses to document inheritance patterns
Compare with non-farmer occupations (laborers, artisans) to quantify economic advantage of farming status

Cross-References
Related Occupational Pages

Miller Occupation Profile — Occupational decline narrative (James Bellamy case study)
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns — Aggregate occupational analysis

Related Geographic Pages

Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (primary Iowa farming hub)
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center (Minnesota farming consolidation)
Eau Claire County, Wisconsin — Bangle Family Settlement Center (Wisconsin farmer settlement)

Related Family Pages

Ault Tallman Branch Summary (Ault farmer legacy)
Palmer Prior Lewis Branch Summary (Lewis farmer migration arc)

Individual Farmer Pages

Nathan Spicer — Spicer farming dynasty founder
Charles Russell Spicer — Complex occupational transitions
George B. Spicer — Established Iowa farmer
